justifications.text: Jewelry consumption is the largest single component of physical gold demand; higher jewelry demand directly increases total demand and supports gold prices, while high prices also feed back to suppress price-sensitive jewelry buyers.

Unemployment is one of the two primary inputs into Fed policy under the dual mandate; rising unemployment historically triggers Fed rate cuts (lower Fed Funds), while labor-market tightness contributes to rate hikes.

The unemployment rate is a primary input into Fed monetary policy under its dual mandate; rising unemployment causes the Fed to cut rates, while falling unemployment permits or necessitates tightening.
Under the Fed's dual mandate, rising unemployment signals labor-market weakness that prompts monetary easing (lower Fed Funds), while falling unemployment toward full employment supports tightening.

The Fed's dual mandate includes maximum employment; rising unemployment typically causes the Fed to lower the Fed Funds rate to stimulate economic activity, while falling unemployment removes the constraint on rate increases.

The Federal Reserve's dual mandate includes maximum employment; rising unemployment is a primary trigger for Fed rate cuts, while falling unemployment toward full employment allows or prompts rate increases.

Global economic expansion supports rising household incomes particularly in key jewelry-demand markets like India and China, driving higher consumer spending on gold jewelry.

High unemployment directly affects consumer economic pessimism — households experiencing or fearing job losses are more likely to expect sustained bad economic times, directly feeding the Michigan pessimism measure.

High or rising unemployment directly shapes household expectations about future economic conditions; when unemployment rises, consumers rationally update toward more pessimistic multi-year economic outlooks.

Rising unemployment directly increases consumer pessimism about future economic conditions, as households facing job-loss risk or observing layoffs form more negative multi-year economic expectations.

Rising unemployment directly increases consumer pessimism about future multi-year economic conditions as households observe job losses and rising economic insecurity in their communities.

Global economic activity is the primary driver of crude oil demand; an improving CLI signals stronger industrial and transportation activity, increasing oil demand and supporting prices.

Global economic activity is the primary driver of oil demand; a rising CLI signals accelerating global growth and stronger energy demand, supporting oil prices, while a falling CLI signals demand weakness.

The OECD CLI is designed to lead economic turning points; improving global economic prospects support corporate earnings expectations, positively influencing equity market returns.

The global CLI is a leading indicator of economic activity; improving global economic conditions support corporate earnings expectations and equity returns, while deteriorating CLI signals contraction and weighs on equities.

The CLI is a leading indicator of global economic activity, and improving global growth prospects lift corporate earnings expectations and equity returns, while deteriorating CLI signals recession risk and pressures equities.

The OECD CLI anticipates global economic turning points; improving global growth expectations (rising CLI) support corporate earnings and equity returns, while deteriorating CLI signals weigh on equities.

The global leading indicator anticipates changes in corporate earnings growth and business activity that drive equity valuations; above-trend CLI readings support positive equity returns while deterioration signals earnings headwinds.

Equity markets are forward-looking and respond to leading indicators of global economic activity; a rising CLI signals improving global conditions that support corporate earnings expectations and equity returns.

The OECD CLI is designed to lead business-cycle turning points; improving global economic conditions captured by a rising CLI support corporate earnings and equity returns, while deteriorating CLI signals contraction risk that pressures equities.

Improving global economic conditions captured by the CLI are associated with stronger corporate earnings and equity market returns; the CLI leads turning points in activity that equity markets also anticipate.

The U.S. unemployment rate is a coincident indicator of economic conditions that co-moves with global leading indicators; deteriorating U.S. labor markets are both a symptom of and a contributor to global economic downturns captured by the CLI.

The CLI is designed to lead real-economy turning points; deteriorating global conditions captured by a falling CLI precede rising domestic unemployment, while expansion corresponds to tightening labor markets.
U.S. labor market conditions are a key component of economic cycle assessment; rising unemployment is associated with below-trend global activity captured by the CLI.

Labor-market conditions are a coincident indicator of economic activity that informs the CLI's underlying component data; rising unemployment signals below-trend economic activity consistent with a falling CLI.

The U.S. unemployment rate is a coincident indicator of U.S. economic conditions, and U.S. economic health is a major driver of global business-cycle conditions captured by the OECD CLI; rising unemployment precedes or accompanies CLI deterioration.
The CLI leads turning points in economic activity; declining global economic momentum typically precedes rising unemployment as labor demand weakens.

VIX and equity returns are mechanically and behaviorally linked: large negative S&P 500 returns drive VIX spikes (the leverage effect), and elevated VIX itself reflects and amplifies equity-market selling pressure.
VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and spikes during equity market selloffs; contemporaneously, high VIX periods correspond to negative equity returns and vice versa.
Large negative equity returns mechanically increase implied volatility and fear, driving VIX higher; the leverage effect and investor panic during drawdowns directly drive the VIX level.

VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and mechanically inversely related to equity returns; large negative equity returns spike VIX, and elevated VIX reflects anticipation of further equity-market turbulence.
VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and spikes contemporaneously with large negative equity returns; the two variables are mechanically and economically intertwined through the equity market.
VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and spikes during equity market sell-offs, creating a mechanical negative contemporaneous relationship with S&P 500 returns.

VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and mechanically reflects realized equity-market volatility; large negative equity returns cause VIX to spike, and elevated VIX discourages risk-taking and suppresses equity returns.
VIX spikes are mechanically and causally linked to equity selloffs; rising implied volatility reflects and reinforces negative equity returns through options pricing and investor de-risking behavior.

VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and spikes when equity markets sell off sharply; large negative S&P 500 returns mechanically increase implied volatility and investor demand for downside protection, driving VIX higher.

VIX is derived from S&P 500 option prices and spikes during sharp equity sell-offs; negative S&P 500 returns mechanically cause VIX to rise through the leverage effect and increased demand for protective puts.

Realized inflation affects real yields through both the Fisher relationship (nominal yields adjust to inflation) and through the Fed's policy response; high inflation that is not fully reflected in nominal yields compresses real yields.
Realized inflation influences market expectations about future Fed policy and the real return environment, shifting real yields as investors reprice TIPS against nominal bonds.

Realized inflation affects the real yield through its impact on monetary policy expectations and on the nominal-real decomposition of Treasury yields; higher inflation can reduce ex-post real yields and shift TIPS pricing.

Realized inflation affects the pricing of TIPS and influences the spread between nominal and real yields; changes in realized inflation feed into TIPS breakeven and real yield dynamics through the Fisher equation decomposition.

Realized inflation affects real yields by influencing both the inflation expectations embedded in nominal yields and the Fed's policy response; sustained high inflation that prompts policy tightening raises real yields.
Higher realized inflation erodes the real value of nominal bonds, prompting portfolio reallocation toward TIPS and affecting real yield levels through supply-demand dynamics in the TIPS market.
Higher realized inflation can compress or shift real yields as it feeds into nominal yield adjustments and TIPS breakeven pricing; persistent inflation surprises alter the real yield level.

Realized inflation feeds into investor inflation expectations and monetary policy responses; the Fed tightens in response to high CPI, raising both nominal and real yields, with the real yield effect depending on whether nominal rates rise faster than inflation expectations.

Realized inflation affects the ex-ante real yield through the Fisher relationship and through the Fed's policy response; high inflation accompanied by rate hikes pushes up real yields as nominal rates rise faster than inflation expectations.

Higher debt-to-GDP ratios can raise the fiscal risk premium embedded in Treasury yields and contribute to upward pressure on long-term real yields as investors demand greater compensation for holding government debt.
Elevated fiscal deficits and rising debt-to-GDP can push up the term premium on long-dated Treasuries, influencing real yields through increased supply of government debt and sovereign risk concerns.
Rising fiscal debt burdens can push up the term premium and real yields as investors demand higher compensation for sovereign credit and supply risk on Treasury issuance.

Higher government debt increases Treasury supply and raises fiscal-risk premiums, which can push up nominal and real yields as investors demand compensation for holding debt of a more fiscally stressed sovereign.

Rising sovereign debt can put upward pressure on term premiums and real yields as bond markets demand greater compensation for fiscal risk; alternatively, high debt burdens can suppress real yields if financial repression is anticipated.

Higher sovereign debt levels raise concerns about fiscal sustainability and can push real term premia higher as investors demand additional compensation for holding long-duration U.S. government bonds.
Higher government debt burdens can raise term premia on long-dated Treasuries as investors demand greater compensation for fiscal risk, pushing up nominal and real yields on 10-year securities.

Higher debt-to-GDP ratios increase Treasury supply, putting upward pressure on real yields as the government must offer higher returns to attract buyers; this is the standard crowding-out mechanism in bond markets.

Elevated fiscal stress raises the term premium and sovereign risk premium embedded in Treasury bonds, pushing up nominal and real long-term yields through increased supply of government debt and fiscal-sustainability concerns.
Higher U.S. real yields attract global capital seeking real returns, increasing demand for dollar-denominated assets and strengthening the dollar.
Higher U.S. real yields make dollar-denominated safe assets more attractive relative to foreign alternatives, drawing capital into the dollar and strengthening DXY.

Higher real U.S. Treasury yields attract foreign capital seeking superior real returns in dollar assets, increasing demand for USD and strengthening the exchange rate through the capital-flow channel.

Higher real U.S. Treasury yields attract foreign capital into dollar-denominated safe assets, increasing demand for dollars and strengthening the DXY; lower real yields reduce the dollar's safe-asset appeal.
Higher U.S. real yields attract foreign capital seeking positive real returns in dollar assets, increasing demand for dollars and strengthening DXY.
Higher U.S. real yields increase the real return advantage of dollar assets relative to foreign alternatives, attracting capital inflows that strengthen the dollar.
Higher U.S. real yields make dollar-denominated assets more attractive to global investors, increasing capital inflows and supporting dollar strength.
Higher U.S. real yields make dollar-denominated safe assets more attractive to global investors on a real return basis, attracting capital inflows that strengthen the dollar.

Higher U.S. real yields increase the real return on dollar assets relative to foreign assets, attracting international capital flows and strengthening the dollar through the real interest-rate differential channel.

Silver has substantial industrial demand (solar, electronics, EVs) that responds to global economic activity, similar to industrial commodities like oil; shared macro-growth drivers create a causal linkage between silver and oil prices.

Both silver and oil are industrial commodities with prices sensitive to global economic activity and industrial demand; common macro drivers such as global manufacturing cycles create co-movement between them.

Silver has substantial industrial demand (solar panels, electronics) that depends on global economic activity, and both silver and oil are commodities whose prices respond to global growth and industrial output cycles.

Both silver and oil are industrial commodities whose prices respond to global manufacturing activity and economic cycles; shared exposure to industrial demand creates co-movement, and energy is a cost input in silver mining and processing.

Silver has significant industrial demand (solar, electronics, EVs) that is sensitive to global economic activity, and oil price movements affect both silver mining costs and industrial production conditions that drive silver demand.

Both silver and oil have significant industrial demand components and respond to global economic activity; strong industrial output drives simultaneous demand for both commodities through the industrial-activity channel.

Both silver and oil are industrial commodities sensitive to global economic activity; expanding industrial production drives demand for both simultaneously, creating correlated movements driven by the global business cycle and shared macro drivers.

Silver and oil share common macro-financial drivers including inflation expectations and global industrial demand; they tend to co-move in response to shared inflationary shocks and global demand cycle fluctuations.

Major geopolitical events that disrupt trade, trigger sanctions, or require large fiscal responses simultaneously elevate economic policy uncertainty, as governments must respond with uncertain and potentially disruptive policy actions.
Major geopolitical events create policy uncertainty about government responses (sanctions, defense spending, regulatory changes), causing EPU to rise contemporaneously with geopolitical risk spikes.

Major geopolitical events (wars, sanctions regimes, trade conflicts) directly generate economic policy uncertainty as governments respond with new fiscal, trade, and regulatory measures, causing both indices to co-move during acute geopolitical episodes.

Major geopolitical events create domestic policy uncertainty — triggering debates about sanctions, defense spending, trade policy, and fiscal response — causing EPU to rise alongside GPR during international crises.

Major geopolitical events often simultaneously generate domestic economic policy uncertainty as governments respond with sanctions, fiscal measures, and regulatory changes; the two indices frequently spike together during events like the 2022 Russia-Ukraine war.

Major geopolitical events generate policy uncertainty as governments respond with sanctions, military spending, trade restrictions, and emergency fiscal measures, causing both indices to spike together during crisis episodes.

During periods of elevated market stress (high VIX), institutional investors rotate into gold ETFs as safe-haven vehicles, creating a causal link from VIX to ETF inflows; the reverse causality is more tenuous but large ETF flows can signal sentiment shifts.

Elevated market volatility and stress drive institutional and retail investors into gold ETFs as safe-haven vehicles; VIX spikes are consistently associated with gold ETF inflow surges.

High economic policy uncertainty is likely to cause households to be more pessimistic about the five-year economic outlook, as unclear policy direction signals potential instability and adverse economic outcomes.

Elevated economic policy uncertainty depresses equity valuations by raising discount rates and reducing confidence in corporate earnings forecasts, generating negative equity returns.

Higher economic policy uncertainty depresses equity valuations by raising the discount rate applied to future earnings and increasing the probability of adverse regulatory or fiscal outcomes, weighing on equity returns.
High economic policy uncertainty depresses equity valuations by raising discount rates and compressing earnings expectations, weighing on S&P 500 returns.

Improving global economic conditions reduce corporate default risk, compressing credit spreads; deteriorating CLI signals rising recession probability and wider credit spreads.

Improving global economic conditions reduce corporate default risk and compress credit spreads, while deteriorating global conditions raise default risk and widen spreads; CLI leads these credit-cycle dynamics.

Consumer pessimism about multi-year economic conditions reflects and amplifies reduced consumer spending and investment expectations, which negatively affect corporate earnings prospects and equity returns.
Consumer pessimism reflects and reinforces negative economic expectations, weighing on equity market sentiment and contributing to negative equity returns.

Rising real yields reduce the attractiveness of non-yielding gold, prompting outflows from gold ETFs; falling real yields stimulate ETF inflows as the opportunity cost of holding gold declines — real yields are a primary driver of institutional ETF positioning.

Diesel and energy inputs are major variable costs in gold mining operations; high oil prices raise mining costs and can constrain mine production at the margin, creating a causal link from oil to mine production.

Oil and energy are major variable input costs in gold mining operations; higher oil prices raise production costs and can suppress mine production over time as marginal mines become uneconomic.

Energy and diesel costs are major variable inputs in gold mining operations; sustained high oil prices raise production costs sufficiently to make some marginal mines uneconomic, constraining mine output.

Through the Phillips curve mechanism, lower unemployment (tighter labor markets) tends to generate wage and price inflation, while higher unemployment creates disinflationary pressure — a standard macro relationship linking labor markets to inflation.

Unemployment and inflation are linked through the Phillips curve mechanism; lower unemployment tightens labor markets and generates wage-push inflation, while higher unemployment reduces inflationary pressure.

Labor-market slack (high unemployment) reduces wage growth and consumer spending, exerting downward pressure on consumer prices through the Phillips-curve mechanism; tight labor markets do the reverse.

The Phillips curve relationship links unemployment to inflation; low unemployment tightens labor markets, puts upward pressure on wages and prices, and feeds through to higher CPI inflation, while high unemployment depresses inflation.
Geopolitical stress events often trigger flight-to-dollar safe-haven flows that strengthen the DXY, though severe U.S.-centered geopolitical risk can also weaken confidence in dollar assets.
Geopolitical stress often triggers a flight to dollar-denominated safe assets, strengthening the DXY as global investors seek dollar liquidity during crisis episodes.

Geopolitical stress often triggers safe-haven flows into USD-denominated assets, strengthening the dollar; however, geopolitical events involving the U.S. directly can weaken confidence in the dollar, making the relationship regime-dependent.
Geopolitical stress episodes trigger safe-haven demand for the U.S. dollar as the world's reserve currency, tending to strengthen DXY during acute geopolitical risk episodes.
During geopolitical crises, the dollar tends to strengthen as a global safe-haven currency, attracting international capital flows away from conflict-exposed currencies and assets.
Oil is priced in dollars globally; a stronger dollar makes oil more expensive for foreign buyers, reducing international demand and putting downward pressure on dollar-denominated oil prices.

Oil is priced globally in U.S. dollars; a stronger dollar makes oil more expensive for non-dollar buyers, reducing global demand and weighing on oil prices, while a weaker dollar reduces the effective cost of oil for foreign buyers.

During risk-off episodes when VIX spikes, investors flight-to-safety into U.S. dollar assets, typically strengthening the dollar index as a reserve-currency safe haven.
During risk-off episodes that elevate VIX, investors seek safety in U.S. dollar assets, strengthening the dollar (flight-to-dollar phenomenon alongside flight-to-gold).
During risk-off episodes when VIX spikes, global investors flee to the dollar as a safe-haven currency, increasing demand for USD and strengthening the DXY.

During risk-off episodes that spike VIX, the U.S. dollar typically strengthens as a safe-haven currency as global capital flows into dollar-denominated assets, creating a positive relationship between financial stress and dollar strength.

During risk-off episodes when VIX spikes, investors flight-to-safety into dollar-denominated assets, increasing demand for the dollar and strengthening the DXY through the safe-haven currency channel.
Geopolitical stress raises systemic financial risk and uncertainty about corporate operating environments, widening credit spreads as investors demand higher compensation for holding corporate debt.
Geopolitical stress episodes raise uncertainty about corporate operating environments, supply chains, and counterparty risks, causing credit spreads to widen as risk premia expand.

Geopolitical stress raises uncertainty about corporate earnings and supply chains, widening credit spreads as investors demand greater compensation for holding corporate debt during conflict or sanctions episodes.

Geopolitical risk events raise systemic uncertainty and risk aversion, causing investors to demand higher compensation for holding corporate credit risk, which widens IG credit spreads.
Policy uncertainty drives institutional investors toward gold ETFs as a hedge against policy regime risk, increasing ETF inflows during high-EPU episodes.

Policy uncertainty drives institutional investors toward defensive assets including gold ETFs as a hedge against uncertain policy regimes affecting equity and bond markets.

Higher unemployment reduces household income and consumer confidence, suppressing discretionary spending on jewelry and reducing physical gold demand through this channel.
Rising unemployment reduces household incomes and discretionary spending power, directly reducing consumer demand for gold jewelry, especially in price-sensitive markets.

Rising unemployment signals weaker economic conditions and reduced corporate earnings expectations, typically weighing on equity returns; sharp unemployment increases often accompany equity bear markets.

Rising unemployment signals weaker corporate earnings prospects and recession risk, weighing on equity returns; conversely, strong equity markets partially reflect strong labor demand and low unemployment expectations.

Rising unemployment signals deteriorating economic conditions and corporate earnings, weighing on equity valuations and returns; improving labor markets support earnings growth and equity performance.

Rising unemployment signals weakening corporate earnings through reduced consumer spending, weighing on equity valuations and generating negative equity returns; falling unemployment has the opposite effect.

Central-bank gold purchases funded by selling dollar reserves directly reduce demand for U.S. dollar assets, exerting downward pressure on the DXY; the post-2022 wave of de-dollarization-motivated gold buying is a clear mechanism.

Central bank gold purchases often reflect diversification away from U.S. dollar reserves; large-scale official-sector buying of gold funded by selling dollar reserves exerts downward pressure on the dollar's value.

High and rising government debt burdens generate policy uncertainty about future fiscal adjustments (tax increases, spending cuts, potential monetization), contributing to elevated economic policy uncertainty.

High and rising government debt levels create uncertainty about future fiscal policy paths — potential tax increases, spending cuts, or monetization — contributing to elevated economic policy uncertainty.

Deteriorating fiscal sustainability can spill over into broader credit-market stress, as higher sovereign debt loads raise systemic risk perceptions and widen corporate credit spreads through contagion.
Consumer pessimism about multi-year economic conditions drives retail and household investors toward defensive assets including gold ETFs, increasing net inflows during high-pessimism periods.
Pessimistic consumer expectations about multi-year economic conditions drive retail investors toward defensive gold ETF holdings as a store of value during anticipated bad times.

Consumer pessimism about economic prospects drives retail and institutional investors toward defensive investment vehicles including gold ETFs, increasing net inflows as part of the same flight-to-safety behavior.

A weaker dollar raises the cost of imported goods and contributes to domestic inflation expectations; conversely, dollar appreciation can suppress inflation expectations by reducing import-price pressures.

Rising U.S. debt-to-GDP motivates foreign central banks to diversify reserve holdings away from dollar-denominated Treasuries toward gold, increasing official-sector gold purchases.

Rising U.S. debt-to-GDP motivates foreign central banks to diversify their reserve portfolios away from U.S. Treasuries and into gold, as high U.S. indebtedness raises long-run dollar debasement concerns for reserve managers.

Both central-bank purchases and ETF flows represent investment demand channels for gold that respond to overlapping macro drivers (inflation fears, dollar weakness, geopolitical risk); large central-bank buying can also signal gold's safe-asset status, reinforcing institutional ETF demand.

Both ETF flows and central-bank purchases respond to common drivers of institutional gold demand — inflation fears, geopolitical risk, dollar weakness — and may reinforce each other as synchronized demand shocks in the physical gold market.

High realized inflation erodes household purchasing power and is one of the most direct drivers of consumer pessimism; persistently elevated CPI causes more survey respondents to expect bad economic times ahead.
High realized inflation reduces the real purchasing power of households and raises economic anxiety, increasing the share of consumers who expect bad economic times in the Michigan survey.

A rapidly rising debt-to-GDP ratio can erode foreign investor confidence in the long-run value of the dollar, generating selling pressure on the DXY as reserve managers diversify away from dollar assets.

Deteriorating U.S. fiscal sustainability signals long-run dollar debasement risk, which tends to weaken the dollar as foreign investors demand higher risk premiums on dollar-denominated assets or diversify away from them.

A stronger dollar reduces the purchasing power of non-dollar consumers for dollar-priced gold, suppressing jewelry demand in key markets like India and China where incomes are denominated in local currencies.
A stronger dollar increases the local-currency cost of gold for consumers in India, China, and other major jewelry markets, reducing purchasing power and dampening jewelry demand in those regions.

Like gold, silver is priced in U.S. dollars on global markets; dollar strength makes silver more expensive for non-dollar buyers and reduces global demand, while dollar weakness provides the same demand-support channel as for gold.

Silver is priced in USD on global markets; dollar strength reduces silver's affordability for foreign buyers, suppressing demand, while dollar weakness supports silver prices through the same currency-denomination mechanism as gold.

During global expansions, institutional investors reduce defensive gold ETF holdings and rotate into growth assets; during contractions, they increase gold ETF allocations as a safe-haven hedge, making ETF flows systematically responsive to the global cycle.

During global economic expansion, risk appetite rises and investors rotate away from defensive assets like gold ETFs, causing outflows; during contraction, risk aversion rises and ETF inflows increase.

Precious-metals ETF flows reflect broader investor sentiment toward the precious metals complex; strong gold ETF inflows signal risk-off or inflation-hedge demand that simultaneously drives silver ETF buying, transmitting common demand shocks across both metals.

Silver ETF flows and gold ETF flows are driven by overlapping macro factors (inflation expectations, risk aversion, monetary policy); institutional investors often rotate between gold and silver ETFs simultaneously, creating correlated flow patterns.
Equity market selloffs trigger risk-off reallocation from equities into safe-haven assets including gold ETFs, while equity rallies reduce the relative attractiveness of defensive gold allocations.

Negative equity returns prompt institutional investors to rotate into defensive gold ETF positions as part of risk-off portfolio rebalancing, while strong equity returns draw capital away from gold ETFs toward growth assets.
Rising policy rates tighten financial conditions and increase corporate borrowing costs, widening credit spreads as refinancing risk and default probability increase for leveraged issuers.

Tighter monetary policy raises financing costs for corporations and slows economic activity, increasing default risk and widening credit spreads; easing has the reverse effect through the credit and liquidity channels.

Consumer pessimism and financial-market stress measures tend to co-move during recessionary episodes; elevated household pessimism both reflects and reinforces risk-averse behavior that contributes to equity-market volatility.

Consumer pessimism and financial-market stress are driven by overlapping macroeconomic anxieties; periods of elevated VIX and financial stress typically coincide with deteriorating consumer expectations, and vice versa, through shared uncertainty channels.

Rising inflation expectations motivate institutional investors to add inflation-hedge exposure through gold ETFs, generating net inflows; falling inflation expectations reduce the inflation-hedge appeal of gold and can trigger outflows.
High economic policy uncertainty, particularly around U.S. fiscal and monetary policy, motivates central banks to diversify reserves away from dollar-denominated assets toward gold.

Periods of elevated financial market stress increase the strategic motivation for central banks to accumulate gold as a reserve asset uncorrelated with financial-system stress, though this channel operates with longer lags than private-sector responses.

High consumer price inflation erodes real household purchasing power in key jewelry-consuming markets, reducing discretionary spending on gold jewelry, particularly in price-sensitive emerging-market consumers.
Above-trend global economic activity tightens product and labor markets, creating inflationary pressures that feed through to realized CPI inflation; below-trend activity does the reverse.

High and rising sovereign debt burdens constrain the Fed's ability to raise rates aggressively (since higher rates raise debt-servicing costs and can trigger fiscal crises), creating a political-economy pressure on monetary policy that links fiscal and monetary variables.

U.S.-specific policy uncertainty can weaken confidence in U.S. dollar assets and trigger capital outflows, affecting the dollar exchange rate; major trade-policy uncertainty (tariffs) has historically correlated with dollar movements.
Geopolitical risk events motivate institutional and retail investors to add gold exposure through ETFs as a safe-haven vehicle, generating net inflows during periods of elevated geopolitical stress.

Institutional investors who shift into gold ETFs during credit stress episodes are simultaneously reducing exposure to credit-sensitive assets; ETF inflows and widening credit spreads co-occur as part of the same risk-off reallocation.

Geopolitical risk events create uncertainty about global economic conditions and corporate earnings, typically weighing on equity returns as investors de-risk portfolios during periods of elevated international tensions.
Silver and gold are frequently co-produced from the same polymetallic deposits; changes in mining activity driven by one metal's economics affect the joint supply of both metals.
Silver has significant industrial demand (electronics, solar panels, EVs); a rising global CLI signals stronger industrial output and manufacturing, increasing silver demand and prices.

Deteriorating global economic momentum (falling CLI) raises equity-market uncertainty and risk aversion, contributing to VIX elevation as investors anticipate weaker corporate earnings and economic stress.

Rising unemployment signals economic weakness, which tends to reduce real interest rates as the Fed eases policy and as the natural rate of interest falls during recessions, transmitting labor market conditions to real yields.

Silver's large industrial-demand component (~50% of total) makes it sensitive to global economic conditions; equity returns and silver returns share common industrial-activity drivers that create a causal linkage through the business cycle.
